beat_prose_text: For more than a year, a ticket that pays off if France wins the World Cup cost about forty cents. 2 Here is the deal on every ticket like it: it pays one dollar if its team wins, and nothing if it does not. So a price of forty cents means the crowd thought France's chance was about forty out of a hundred. The market's own word for a ticket like this is a contract. On July 14, Spain beat France in ninety minutes, and the ticket fell to zero. 1 Watch the white line on the chart ahead: it is France's price through that night, and it falls twice. Each fall is a Spain goal, one in each half, the first from the penalty spot. Down here, one cyan dot is one real trade from that night, and every trade has two sides: one buyer said yes, France wins, and another said no. At the final whistle, the exchange pays a dollar to every winning ticket and nothing to every losing one, a moment traders call settling, and the market closes for good. That is the grey pour you see hit the floor on screen. This was the night Spain booked tonight's final. Before you judge tonight's price, you should meet the thing that set it. The story starts fourteen months earlier.
chip_or_key_text:
cyan = one dot, one real trade
pale grey = money at rest, the whole tournament
grain_plate_text: 1 dot = 1 trade · showing every 102nd of 35,196 trades · France–Spain, July 14

beat_prose_text: This morning, before kickoff, this market froze its price for tonight’s final. Spain’s ticket to lift the trophy cost 58.8 cents. Argentina’s cost 42.0. 23 Strip out the fee, devigged, and Spain’s true chance was 53.9 in 100. Argentina’s was 38.5. Spain won. At the final trade, Spain’s ticket settled near 99.9 cents. Argentina’s settled near 0.1. 24 The frozen number was not a guess. It was a price, and the price paid.
chip_or_key_text:
crimson = Spain, this morning and at the whistle
grey = Argentina, this morning and at the whistle
amber = the frozen call that paid
grey dots = money at rest, the whole tournament
grain_plate_text: 1 dot = $75,000 traded again · this is the whole tournament · it never leaves

beat_prose_text: Ninety minutes ended level. Spain became champion in extra time, not in regulation. 24 This piece taught that lesson once already, at the Germany-Paraguay shootout: check which ticket is talking. Tonight two tickets disagreed, and a reader who learned that lesson read both of them right. The trophy ticket said Spain would win the World Cup. A second ticket asked a narrower question: would the match still be level after ninety minutes? Before kickoff it traded near 33 cents. As the draw held, it climbed. In the half hour before the whistle on regulation it traded 16,961 times, averaging 85 cents. 24 It settled at 99. A piece called Regulation Time was decided beyond regulation time.
chip_or_key_text:
white = the level-after-ninety ticket, minute by minute
amber = the half hour the draw hardened
grey dots = money at rest, the whole tournament
grain_plate_text: 1 dot = $75,000 traded again · this is the whole tournament · it never leaves

beat_prose_text: Now grade the deepest read of all. At five checkpoints across thirteen months, devigged, this market priced France a few points above a computer model’s guess. It priced Spain a few points below that same guess, at four checkpoints out of five. 21 Tonight the discounted team is the champion. One tournament is one result. It cannot prove thirteen months of pricing right or wrong by itself, and this piece will not pretend otherwise. But the market’s longest-held opinion was also its wrongest one, and that is worth sitting with.
chip_or_key_text:
crimson = Spain's gap to the model
grey dashes = France, for reference
amber = the verdict, graded tonight
grey dots = money at rest, the whole tournament
grain_plate_text: 1 dot = $75,000 traded again · this is the whole tournament · it never leaves

beat_prose_text: About ten minutes after Spain’s coronation settled, Kalshi opened a market on who wins the 2030 World Cup. 25 Eighty-two countries, priced before the confetti finished falling. Spain’s ticket was already the loudest of them, about seven times the volume of the next-biggest country. 25 The next belief was already trading before this one finished being read. Every market above, and this new one too, is still yours to check. The lab is open below.

grey = the other 2030 tickets
amber = already the loudest of 82
grey dots = money at rest, the whole tournament
